courses.course_name: Business Decision Making for Aging Services

sections.notes: This is a fully online course. It requires reliable internet access and computer literacy; and the ability to self-manage independent learning assignments according to deadlines. In place of classroom activities, online work may include independent mastery assignments, discussion boards, videos with quizzes, writing assignments, and other active learning experiences. Students are responsible for completing these online learning experiences by the specified deadlines and in accordance with instructor requirements.

courses.course_name: Honors Thesis in Ancient Studies II

sections.notes: Course description: The anthropology of performance examines how embodied practices such as ritual, dance, theater, music, storytelling, fashion, and everyday social interaction, express and shape cultural, political, and historical life. Anthropologists study performance not only as artistic expression but also as a site of meaning-making, identity formation, and social transformation. This course introduces students to key concepts, debates, and ethnographic approaches in the anthropology of performance. Through close engagement with ethnographies, theoretical texts, and multimedia materials, students will analyze how performance illuminates issues of race, gender, sexuality, class, nationalism, religion, colonialism, and globalization.
Also listed as MLL 602-01.

sections.notes: Topic Description Women in the History of Art: This course explores the roles that women have played in the visual arts as subjects, artists, patrons, partners, critics and historians from antiquity to the present. Although organized in a chronological format, this course will examine women from various parts of the world.

sections.topic: Art & Science in Early Mod Eu
sections.notes: Topic Description: As pioneers of new ways of seeing the world and new technologies to represent it, early modern European artists did not just represent the world, they remade it. This course seeks to understand how new social and economic conditions led to new patterns of art making, patronage, and consumption. We particularly analyze the rise of optics in formulating universal truths, the display of Nature through an expanded book culture, and new beliefs about human society to understand the birth of modernity between 1400 and 1700.
Email Kelley Yang (kelley7@umbc.edu) to discuss enrollment for this section.

courses.course_name: Topics in Art or Media I

sections.notes: Students will explore both the theory and practice of exhibition design and will have opportunities to apply these concepts by assisting with the installation of the MFA and Senior Exhibitions as part of the CADVC production schedule. They will be introduced to a variety of hardware commonly used in installations and will learn the proper techniques for using them in appropriate situations. Through in-class assignments in the CADVC shop, students will be challenged to problem-solve and produce tangible results. Additionally, they will gain experience with technology-based installations, develop effective strategies for presenting multi-media work, practice researching solutions within defined parameters, and receive instruction in lighting design. The course will provide hands-on instruction in exhibition conception, presentation aesthetics, art handling, hardware improvisation and problem-solving, basic fabrication with readily available materials, lighting design, and essential safety protocols.

Seminar in Art History & Visual Culture

sections.topic: Art & Science in Early Mod Eu

sections.notes: This course explores the complex challenges in animated character performance, design and visual storytelling. Through a sequence of short assignments, students will practice character design, lip sync, acting and more. Students may animate and complete assignments in either 2D, 3D or physical mediums using characters of their own design. Critiques and workshops will focus on effective, believable performance.

This course analyzes the conceptualization of character through choices in clothing. Emphasis will be placed on dress theory, script analysis, historical and artistic research, and drawing the human figure. Some course activities will take place online.
Contact Kelley Yang (kelley7@umbc.edu) for permission to enroll.
Contact Kelley Yang (kelley7@umbc.edu) for permission to enroll.

courses.course_name: Senior Art History Thesis or Curatorial Project

sections.notes: What does it mean to live and create in motion across borders, cultures, and generations? Through an immersive study of film, literature, visual art, social media, and performance, students will uncover how diasporic Asians use cultural production to tell their stories, preserve heritage, and reimagine belonging. We investigate how these creative expressions serve as powerful tools to make sense of migration, hybridity, memory, and place continually crafting identities in flux. Asian Diaspora in Motion invites students to think critically and creatively about identity as it unfolds at the intersection of movement, memory, media, and activism.
If course permission is needed, email Dr. Laila M Shishineh (lailams@umbc.edu). This course is taught fully in person.

If course permission is needed, email Dr. Laila M. Shishineh (lailams@umbc.edu).
